schools.summary: Gill: this is not to be understood of love to all mankind, or to those of the same nation, or who are in a strict natural relation brethren (though they are all in a sense brethren), but of that special love which the saints bear to one another as brethren in Christ; the Vulgate and Syriac add 'in you' or 'among you,' as a church and society of Christians; Paul charging that brotherly love continue (Paul, beginning his closing exhortations, pressing first the grace of 'brotherly love,' the warm special affection that believers bear one another as members of one spiritual family, sons of the same Father and brethren in Christ, and bidding it 'continue,' abide and persevere; he does not bid them begin to love, for they already did, but to keep up and maintain a love already in being, against the things that would cool or break it, so the church's first duty in its life together is the unbroken continuance of brotherly love).

themes: let brotherly love
continue
the special love of the saints as brethren
maintained unbroken against all that would cool it

schools.summary: Gill: by 'strangers' are meant not unconverted men (strangers to God and Christ), nor saints who are pilgrims in this world, but such as are of another country and unknown, and even though wicked men they are not excluded; Paul charging that they not forget to entertain strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares (Paul pressing the grace of hospitality, especially to 'strangers,' travellers and foreigners, the unknown, who in those days of persecution often needed shelter, bidding the saints 'be not forgetful to entertain' them, receive and lodge them gladly, and adding a sweet encouragement from the past: 'for thereby some have entertained angels unawares,' as Abraham and Lot, who, in welcoming travellers, found they had received angels of God all unknowing, so the believer is to keep an open door, knowing that hospitality may bring an unlooked-for blessing).

themes: be not forgetful to entertain strangers
for thereby some have entertained angels unawares
the duty of hospitality to strangers
Abraham and Lot entertaining angels unknowing

schools.summary: Gill: 'them that are in bonds' are not those bound for criminal actions or debt (though such should be remembered and pitied), but such as are in bonds for the sake of Christ and the gospel, which has often been the lot of God's people, who should be remembered by praying for them and sending comfortable supplies; Paul charging that they remember those in bonds as bound with them, and those who suffer adversity as being themselves in the body (Paul calling for a tender sympathy with the persecuted: 'remember them that are in bonds,' the prisoners suffering for Christ's sake, and that 'as bound with them,' entering into their case as if sharing their very chains, by prayer, comfort, and aid, and 'them which suffer adversity,' remembering them 'as being yourselves also in the body,' as men still in the body liable to the same sufferings, who may one day need the like compassion, so Christian fellow-feeling is to put itself in the place of the afflicted, sympathizing as if it were our own suffering).

themes: remember them that are in bonds, as bound with them
them which suffer adversity, as being yourselves also in the body
the fellow-feeling with the persecuted and afflicted
entering into others' sufferings as one's own

schools.summary: Gill: 'marriage' is the union of one man and one woman in wedlock, whereby they become one flesh, a joining of male and female of two only and of such as are not within the forbidden degrees; some read these words as an exhortation, 'let it be honourable,' others as an assertion, it is honourable; Paul declaring that marriage is honourable in all and the bed undefiled, but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge (Paul setting the dignity of marriage against all sexual sin: 'marriage is honourable in all,' a holy and honourable estate open to all sorts of men, not to be despised as some falsely taught, 'and the bed undefiled,' the marriage bed pure and chaste with no defilement in the lawful use of it, 'but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge,' those who defile themselves with fornication or break the marriage bond by adultery shall fall under God's righteous judgment, so Paul honouring the marriage God ordained and warning solemnly against the impurity that profanes it).

themes: marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled
but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge
the honour of marriage upheld
the impure threatened with God's judgment

schools.summary: Gill: covetousness is an immoderate desire of riches, an over-anxious care for worldly things, attended with dissatisfaction and discontent with one's present state; it discovers itself in preferring the world to religion and in laying up treasure for a man's own self; Paul charging that their manner of life be without covetousness, content with what they have, since God has said he will never leave nor forsake them (Paul bidding the believers' whole 'conversation,' manner of life, be 'without covetousness,' free from the greedy anxious craving for riches, and instead marked by contentment, 'content with such things as ye have,' and giving the firmest ground for such contentment, God's own promise, 'for he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee'; the strength of this assurance, that since God himself has pledged never to abandon his own, the believer who has God needs nothing else and may rest content in any earthly lot, his portion being the unfailing presence of God).

themes: let your conversation be without covetousness; and be content with such things as ye have
for he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee
contentment grounded in God's unfailing presence
he who has God needs nothing else

schools.summary: Barnes: without any hesitation or doubt, in all times of perplexity and threatening want, in all times when we scarcely know whence the supplies for our necessities are to come, we may put our trust in God; Gill notes nothing is more true than this, that we may confidently assert it; Paul declaring that, so assured, we may boldly say, the Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do (Paul drawing the triumphant conclusion from God's promise: 'so that we may boldly say,' with full confidence and no doubt, 'The Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do unto me'; the reasoning of faith, that since God has pledged never to forsake us and is himself our helper, we are lifted above all fear of man, for what can man do to one whom the Almighty helps, so the believer, leaning on God's promise, can face every threat and every enemy with a holy fearlessness, knowing his Helper is greater than all that man can do).

themes: so that we may boldly say, The Lord is my helper
I will not fear what man shall do unto me
the holy boldness springing from God's promise
faith lifted above all fear of man

schools.summary: Gill: Christ's church is a kingdom, and he is King in it; pastors of churches are subordinate governors, who rule well when they rule not in an arbitrary way according to their own wills but according to the laws of Christ, with all faithfulness, prudence, and diligence; Paul charging that they remember their rulers who have spoken the word of God, follow their faith, and consider the end of their life (Paul bidding the believers honour their spiritual guides: 'remember them which have the rule over you,' the pastors and teachers set over them, perhaps now departed, 'who have spoken unto you the word of God,' who faithfully preached God's truth, and twofold: 'whose faith follow,' imitate their believing, their steadfast trust in God, 'considering the end of their conversation,' marking how their manner of life ended, the blessed issue of their faithful course, perhaps in a holy death or martyrdom, so the saints are to keep their faithful leaders in mind and to copy the faith that carried them through to a good end).

themes: remember them which have the rule over you, who have spoken unto you the word of God
whose faith follow
considering the end of their conversation
honouring leaders, imitating their faith

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is the substance of the word spoken by the above-mentioned rulers, the author and object of their faith, and the end in which their conversation terminated; these words express the duration and immutability of Christ: he was 'yesterday' (not designing the day immediately preceding, but ages past), is the same 'today,' and continues the same 'for ever'; Paul declaring that Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and for ever (Paul, having bidden them follow the faith of their departed leaders, pointing them to the unchanging object of that faith: 'Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever,' the leaders passing away but Christ in whom they trusted abiding forever the same; the fulness of this, that Christ is the same in his person, his love, his power, his promises, and his grace through all time, 'yesterday' the same Saviour the old saints trusted, 'today' the same now to the present church, 'and for ever' unchangeably the same to all eternity, so the believer rests on an immutable Christ who never changes though all else does).

themes: Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day
and for ever
the eternal sameness and immutability of Christ
the anchor of faith though all else changes

schools.summary: Gill: the word 'divers' denotes the variety and multitude of other doctrines, referring either to the various rites and ceremonies of the law, or to the traditions of the elders, or to the several doctrines of men whether Jews or Gentiles, whereas the doctrine of Scripture is uniform; Paul charging that they not be carried about with divers and strange doctrines, but have the heart established with grace, not with meats (Paul, having set the unchanging Christ before them, warning against doctrinal instability: 'be not carried about with divers and strange doctrines,' do not be tossed and swept along by the many novel and alien teachings, especially the Jewish ceremonial notions about foods, for 'it is a good thing that the heart be established with grace,' made firm and stable by the grace of God and the doctrine of grace, 'not with meats, which have not profited them that have been occupied therein'; the contrast, that the old observances about meats and ceremonies never profited those who trusted in them, while grace truly establishes the soul, so the heart is to be grounded on grace not on outward ceremonies).

themes: be not carried about with divers and strange doctrines
it is a good thing that the heart be established with grace
not with meats, which have not profited
the heart grounded on grace, not ceremony

schools.summary: Gill: by 'altar' is meant not the cross of Christ on which he was crucified, nor the Lord's table where his flesh and blood are presented to faith as food (though not offered), but Christ himself, who is altar, sacrifice, and priest; he was typified by the altar of the burnt offering and the sacrifice that was on it; Paul declaring that we have an altar, of which they have no right to eat who serve the tabernacle (Paul setting the believer's spiritual portion against the Jewish ritual: 'we have an altar,' Gill taking this altar to be Christ himself, altar, sacrifice, and priest in one, not a literal table or the cross only, 'whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle,' those who still cling to the legal worship and its sacrifices having no part in this; the meaning that those who rest in the old tabernacle service and refuse Christ have no right to feed by faith on him, the true altar and sacrifice, so the believer's true and abiding altar is Christ, in whom the gospel has a better feast than the law ever gave).

debates.question: What is the 'altar' that Christians have?

sides.position: Christ himself — who is altar, sacrifice, and priest in one, typified by the altar of burnt offering; not a literal table or the cross alone

themes: we have an altar
whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle
the believer's altar in Christ
those clinging to the old tabernacle having no part

schools.summary: Gill: this refers not to the red heifer, nor the sin offering in general, nor those for the priest and people, but to the bullock and goat on the day of atonement, which were typical of Christ, in the bringing of their blood into the most holy place by the high priest for sin and in the burning of their bodies without the camp; Paul observing that the bodies of the beasts whose blood was brought into the sanctuary for sin were burned outside the camp (Paul drawing out a striking detail of the Day of Atonement ritual: 'the bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin,' the bullock and the goat whose blood the high priest carried into the most holy place to make atonement, 'are burned without the camp'; this a deliberate type, the very animals whose blood made atonement having their bodies carried outside the camp and there consumed, so the law itself foreshadowed that the great Sin-bearer, whose blood would make the true atonement, must suffer outside the camp, away from the holy city, as the next verse declares of Christ).

themes: for the bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary
by the high priest for sin
are burned without the camp
the type of the sin-offering pointing to Christ

schools.summary: Barnes: that there might be a conformity between his death for sin and the sacrifices which typified it; it is implied here that it was voluntary on the part of Jesus that he suffered; Gill notes this was in order to answer the type of him; Paul declaring that Jesus, to sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered outside the gate (Paul applying the type to Christ: 'wherefore Jesus also,' to answer the figure of the sin-offering burned outside the camp, 'that he might sanctify the people with his own blood,' to set them apart and cleanse them by his atoning blood, not the blood of beasts, 'suffered without the gate'; the exact correspondence, that as the bodies of the atonement-sacrifices were burned outside the camp, so Christ, the true sacrifice, was led out and crucified outside the gate of Jerusalem, so the very place of his suffering fulfilled the ancient type, and his own blood, shed there, accomplished the sanctification of his people which the beasts' blood only foreshadowed).

themes: wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood
suffered without the gate
Christ fulfilling the type of the sin-offering
sanctifying his people by his own blood

schools.summary: Gill: 'let us go forth unto him without the camp' — either of legal ceremonies, which are to be quitted, or of this world, which may be compared to a camp for its instability (a camp not always in one place) and its hostility (the world being full of enemies to Christ and his people) and its noise and fatigue; Paul charging that we go forth to Christ outside the camp, bearing his reproach (Paul drawing the practical lesson from Christ's suffering outside the gate: 'let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp,' let us leave behind the 'camp,' whether the abandoned system of Jewish ceremonies or the hostile world itself, and go out to Christ where he is, outside the gate, 'bearing his reproach'; the cost and the glory of this, that to follow Christ outside the camp is to share the shame and reproach that fell on him, to be willing to be cast out and despised by the world for his sake as he was, so the believer is called to a decisive separation, leaving the old and the worldly to take his place with the rejected Christ, gladly bearing the reproach that is his honour).

themes: let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp
bearing his reproach
the call to share Christ's reproach outside the camp
a decisive separation to the rejected Christ

schools.summary: Gill: 'here have we no continuing city' — neither for religious worship (the city of Jerusalem being quickly to be destroyed) nor for civil life to dwell in; this may have a peculiar regard to the Hebrews, whose temple and city would in a short time become desolate, though it was the general case of the saints in those times; Paul declaring that here we have no continuing city, but we seek one to come (Paul giving the reason for going out to Christ and bearing reproach: the believer has nothing abiding to lose here, 'for here have we no continuing city,' no permanent home or settled place on earth, least of all the doomed earthly Jerusalem, 'but we seek one to come'; the pilgrim spirit this breeds, that since there is no lasting city below, the heart is set free to seek the one that is to come, the heavenly and abiding city of God, so the loss of earthly things is no real loss to the pilgrim whose true home is yet ahead).

themes: for here have we no continuing city
but we seek one to come
the pilgrim outlook loosening the heart from earth
the heart fixed on the heavenly, abiding city

schools.summary: Gill: this sacrifice of praise is for temporal and spiritual mercies, particularly for sanctification or expiation of sin by the blood of Christ, and for heaven, the continuing city that is to come; this sacrifice is not a proper nor a propitiatory one but spiritual and evangelical, enjoined under the gospel; Paul charging that by Christ we offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, the fruit of our lips giving thanks (Paul, in place of the abolished animal sacrifices, naming the sacrifices that remain under the gospel, beginning with praise: 'by him therefore,' through Christ, the only altar and mediator by whom alone our worship is accepted, 'let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually,' a constant unceasing offering of thanksgiving, 'that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name'; this the Christian's true sacrifice, not the blood of beasts but the heartfelt praise and thanksgiving of redeemed lips, offered through Christ to God, so the believer's continual worship is to bless and thank God for all his mercies, above all for salvation in Christ).

themes: by him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually
that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name
the gospel sacrifice of praise through Christ
the heartfelt thanksgiving of redeemed lips

schools.summary: Gill: this is to be understood not of doing good works in general but of acts of beneficence, or communicating to 'the poor' (as the Syriac renders it); the apostle proceeds to take notice of another sort of sacrifice which continues under the gospel dispensation; Paul charging that they forget not to do good and to communicate, for with such sacrifices God is well pleased (Paul naming a second gospel sacrifice alongside praise: 'but to do good and to communicate forget not,' let them not neglect deeds of kindness, to do good, and the sharing of their goods with the needy, to communicate, giving to the poor, and marking God's delight in such offerings, 'for with such sacrifices God is well pleased'; under the gospel the acceptable sacrifices being not animals but praise to God and goodness to men, and God being as truly pleased with deeds of charity as with worship, so the believer's whole life of thankful praise and generous love is a continual, God-pleasing sacrifice).

themes: but to do good and to communicate forget not
for with such sacrifices God is well pleased
the sacrifice of charity and generosity
charity to men as truly a sacrifice as praise

schools.summary: Chrysostom: anarchy is an evil, and the occasion of many calamities, and the source of disorder and confusion; as, if you take away the leader from a chorus, the chorus will not be in tune and in order, and if from a phalanx of an army you remove the commander the evolutions will no longer be made in time and order, so it is with a flock without its shepherd; Paul charging that they obey and submit to their rulers, who watch for their souls as those that must give account (Paul pressing the duty toward faithful spiritual guides: 'obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves,' yield a dutiful obedience and submission to their godly leadership, and giving the weighty reason, 'for they watch for your souls,' the pastors keeping a sleepless guard over the souls of their flock, caring for their eternal welfare, 'as they that must give account,' knowing they must one day answer to God for those souls, 'that they may do it with joy, and not with grief: for that is unprofitable for you'; the people's obedience making the pastors' watch a joy, while their disobedience makes it a grief, and the loss of such grief falling on the people themselves).

themes: obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves
for they watch for your souls, as they that must give account
that they may do it with joy, and not with grief
obedience making the pastors' watch a joy

schools.summary: Gill: 'pray for us' — for those in the ministry, your guides and governors, since the work is of so much moment, and so arduous and awful, and you have such a concern in it; Matthew Henry: the apostle recommends himself and his fellow-sufferers to the prayers of the Hebrew believers; Paul asking the believers to pray for him and his fellow-ministers, since they trust they have a good conscience, willing to live honestly (Paul, having charged the people's duty to their leaders, asking in turn for their prayers: 'pray for us,' for him and his fellow-labourers in the gospel, and commending himself to their confidence by his integrity: 'for we trust we have a good conscience,' a conscience clear of guile and hypocrisy before God and men, 'in all things willing to live honestly,' sincerely desiring to walk uprightly in everything; Paul, conscious of his own honest and upright aim, the more freely asking their prayers, since they could pray for one whose conscience and conduct they could trust).

themes: pray for us
for we trust we have a good conscience
in all things willing to live honestly
the request for prayer grounded in integrity

schools.summary: Barnes: it is here clearly implied that the writer was deterred from visiting them by some adverse circumstances over which he had no control, which might be either by imprisonment or by other hindrances; Gill notes he beseeches them to pray earnestly, to strive together in their prayers for them; Paul beseeching them the more earnestly to pray that he might be restored to them sooner (Paul pressing his request for prayer with a personal earnestness: 'but I beseech you the rather to do this,' to pray for him all the more, 'that I may be restored to you the sooner'; what this reveals, that Paul was being kept from them by some hindrance beyond his control, likely his imprisonment, and he so longed to be with them and so trusted the power of their prayers that he begged them to pray the harder, believing that their intercession might hasten his release and reunion with them, so he counted the prayers of the saints a real means by which God might bring about his return).

themes: but I beseech you the rather to do this
that I may be restored to you the sooner
the urgent plea for prayer toward reunion
counting the saints' prayers a real means of return

schools.summary: Gill: this is the concluding part of the epistle, ended with a prayer made up of very suitable petitions for the Hebrews; the apostle addresses God as 'the God of peace,' who is so called because he is the author of peace, of reconciliation by Christ; Paul opening his closing prayer by addressing the God of peace who brought again from the dead the Lord Jesus, the great shepherd, through the blood of the everlasting covenant (Paul, as he asked their prayers, now praying for them, beginning with a rich naming of God: 'the God of peace,' the author of all reconciliation and peace through Christ, 'that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus,' who raised Christ from the grave, the great proof of his accepted sacrifice, 'that great shepherd of the sheep,' Christ the chief and good Shepherd who laid down his life and rose for his flock, 'through the blood of the everlasting covenant'; the resurrection of Christ resting on the merit of his blood, the blood that ratified the everlasting covenant of grace, so the very ground of the believer's hope is in this risen Shepherd and his covenant blood).

themes: now the God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus
that great shepherd of the sheep
through the blood of the everlasting covenant
the believer's hope in the risen Shepherd

schools.summary: Gill: a good work is what is done in obedience to the command of God, and in faith, and from a principle of love, in the name and strength of Christ, and with a view to the glory of God (the Alexandrian copy reads 'in every good work and word'); Paul praying that God make them perfect in every good work to do his will, working in them what is well-pleasing through Jesus Christ (Paul naming the great request of his benediction: that this God of peace would 'make you perfect in every good work to do his will,' fully fit, equip, and furnish them for all obedience, and the secret of it, 'working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight,' God himself working within them the very obedience he requires, 'through Jesus Christ'; the precious truth here, that the good works God requires he himself works in his people, so all our doing of his will is the fruit of his own gracious operation within us through Christ, and Paul closing with a doxology, 'to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen').

themes: make you perfect in every good work to do his will
working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ
to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen
the good works God requires he himself works

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle closes his epistle to the Hebrews in a very tender and affectionate manner, calling them 'brethren' as he often does, and speaking to them not in an authoritative way nor by way of advice but by entreaty (the reason perhaps being that he was the apostle of the Gentiles); Paul beseeching the brethren to bear with the word of exhortation, since he had written to them in few words (Paul, drawing to a close, making a humble request: 'I beseech you, brethren, suffer the word of exhortation,' bear with, receive patiently and kindly, the exhortation and admonition of this whole letter, and softening it with a gracious reason: 'for I have written a letter unto you in few words,' the letter being, considering its great themes, comparatively brief; the apostle's tenderness, that though he had spoken weighty and searching things he asks not as a commander but as a brother that they take his words in good part, all the more readily because he had been so brief, so the spirit of true exhortation is humble and affectionate).

themes: and I beseech you, brethren, suffer the word of exhortation
for I have written a letter unto you in few words
the gentle plea to receive the exhortation
asking not as a commander but as a brother

schools.summary: Gill: this is the same Timothy to whom the Apostle Paul wrote two epistles, and whom he often calls his son, though sometimes his brother as here; of him he says that he was set at liberty, or 'dismissed,' either by the apostle (by whom he was sent into some parts on some business) or from imprisonment; Paul telling them that brother Timothy is set at liberty, with whom, if he comes shortly, he will see them (Paul sharing a piece of glad news and a hope: 'know ye that our brother Timothy is set at liberty,' Timothy, his beloved fellow-worker, has been released, having himself been imprisoned for the gospel, and looking forward to coming with him, 'with whom, if he come shortly, I will see you'; the warm fellowship of the gospel ministers, that Paul rejoices in Timothy's freedom and plans, should Timothy come soon, to visit the Hebrews together with him, so personal love and the bonds of ministry run through even the close of this great doctrinal letter).

themes: know ye that our brother Timothy is set at liberty
with whom, if he come shortly, I will see you
the news of Timothy's release
the warm fellowship of gospel ministers

schools.summary: Gill: 'salute all them that have the rule over you' are the pastors and officers of the church of the Hebrews, and 'all the saints' the several members of the church, set apart to holiness by God, whose sins were expiated by Christ, to whom Christ was made sanctification, and who were internally sanctified; Paul sending greetings to all their rulers and all the saints, with the salutation of those of Italy (Paul ending with the warm courtesy of Christian love: 'salute all them that have the rule over you,' greet the pastors and leaders of the church on his behalf, 'and all the saints,' every member of the holy company, and conveying greetings from the believers where he was, 'they of Italy salute you,' the Christians of Italy sending their love to the Hebrews; this gracious exchange of greetings between churches and saints far apart, the bond of love in Christ uniting believers across distance).

themes: salute all them that have the rule over you, and all the saints
they of Italy salute you
the closing exchange of Christian greetings
the bond of love uniting believers across distance

schools.summary: Gill: this is the apostle's usual salutation in all his epistles, in which he wishes renewed discoveries of the love and favour of God, fresh communications of grace from Christ, an abundant supply of the spirit of grace, an increase of internal grace and external gifts, and a continuance of the gospel; Paul closing the epistle with the benediction, 'Grace be with you all. Amen' (Paul, as in all his letters, sealing this great epistle with his accustomed and beloved benediction: 'Grace be with you all,' the invoking upon every reader, without exception, 'you all,' of the free favour and grace of God in Christ, with all its fruits and blessings, and setting the solemn 'Amen' upon it; the fitness of this close, that a letter so full of grace, of Christ, and of the better covenant ends, as it should, with the prayer that grace itself might rest and abide upon all the people of God, so the last word of Hebrews is grace).

themes: Grace be with you all
Amen
the final apostolic benediction of grace
a letter full of grace ending with grace
